name: AI Core
description: AI orchestration, model management, and nsfw_ai_server connectivity for Cove.
author: skier233

tutorialTopics.title: AI
tutorialTopics.description: What Cove's AI can do for your library, and how the pieces fit together.

slides.imageAlt: The Run AI dialog where you queue analysis
slides.title: What Cove's AI gives you
slides.caption: The AI extensions read your media and add things you can actually use: searchable tags with timing, people recognized across your library, and search by what something looks or sounds like.
slides.bodyMarkdown:
Each extension unlocks a different superpower:

- **AI Tagging** adds tags that know *when* and *how long* they appear, so they can show up as segments on the player bar and you can filter by how long a tag lasts.
- **AI Faces** recognizes people and helps you link them to performers.
- **AI Visual** lets you search by *meaning* ("sunset on a beach") and jump to look‑alike videos and images.
- **AI Audio** finds videos that sound alike.

They all run through **AI Core**, which talks to the AI server and queues the work.
points: Install AI Core first — every other AI extension needs it
Then enable just the capabilities you want
Run on a small selection first so you can review before a big cleanup

slides.title: Everything starts with Run AI
slides.caption: Select one or more videos or images, then use the Run AI action to queue analysis. It appears on the toolbar of a detail page and in the bulk actions bar when you select multiple items.
slides.bodyMarkdown: In the Run AI dialog you pick which capabilities to run — tagging, faces, visual, audio — based on the extensions you have enabled.
slides.imageAlt: The Run AI dialog with capability checkboxes for tagging, faces, visual, and audio

tutorialTopics.title: AI Core Setup
tutorialTopics.description: One-time setup: connect the AI server, map your paths, and load models.

slides.imageAlt: AI Core settings where you connect the AI server
slides.title: Before you start
slides.caption: AI Core does not run the models itself — it connects to a running AI server (nsfw_ai_server). You need that server reachable from Cove before anything will work.
slides.bodyMarkdown:
You will need:

- A running **AI server** that Cove can reach over the network (local or remote).
- Enough hardware for the models you plan to use — a GPU makes large runs far faster.

If you do not have the server yet, set it up first, then come back here.

slides.title: Connect the AI server
slides.caption: Open Settings → Extensions → AI → AI Core and enter your server URL, then run the health check to confirm Cove can reach it.
slides.bodyMarkdown: Paste your server address (for example `http://127.0.0.1:8000`) into the **Server URL** field and check health. A green/healthy result means you are connected and Cove can see which models are available.
slides.imageAlt: AI Core settings with the Server URL field and health check result
points: [green] Server URL — where your AI server lives
[blue] Health check — confirms Cove can reach it and lists models

slides.title: Map your media paths
slides.caption: If Cove and the AI server see your files under different folder paths, add a path mapping so the server can open the same media Cove scanned.
slides.bodyMarkdown: Add a mapping from the path Cove uses (the **from** prefix) to the path the server uses (the **to** prefix). This is common when the server runs in a container or on another machine. If both run on the same machine with the same paths, you can skip this.
slides.imageAlt: AI Core path mappings table with a from-prefix and to-prefix row

slides.title: Load models and you're ready
slides.caption: Open the model catalog, load the models you want, and AI Core will use them for the matching capabilities.
slides.bodyMarkdown: Loaded models stay ready for runs. Once at least one model is loaded, you can run AI on a small selection to confirm everything works end to end before doing a large batch.
slides.imageAlt: AI Core model catalog with a model being loaded
AI Core model catalog used during a first run
slides.title: First run & troubleshooting
slides.caption: Run AI on a handful of items first. If a run stalls or fails, the usual causes are the server URL, path mappings, or a model that isn't loaded.
slides.bodyMarkdown:
Good first checks when something doesn't work:

- Re-run the **health check** — is the server still reachable?
- Does the failing item's path resolve through your **path mappings**?
- Is the right **model loaded** for the capability you ran?
- For large libraries, runs are queued — give them time, and start small.

tutorialTopics.title: Running AI
tutorialTopics.description: How to run AI on your media and review the results.

slides.title: Run AI on what you've selected
slides.caption: From a video or image detail page use the Run AI toolbar action; on a list page, select multiple items and use Run AI in the bulk actions bar.
slides.bodyMarkdown: Pick the capabilities to run in the dialog. What you can choose depends on which AI extensions you have enabled — tagging, faces, visual, audio.
slides.imageAlt: Selecting videos and opening the Run AI dialog from the bulk actions bar
The Run AI dialog, where you review before committing
slides.title: Review before you commit
slides.caption: Start with a small selection and look at the results before running across your whole library.
slides.bodyMarkdown: Tags appear, faces collect on the Faces page for linking, and visual/audio similarity shows up on detail pages. Once you verify things are working, run for larger batches.
